paragraphs.body: Title: Between name and reality: the surprising crossroads of Shenley Park Main points: By comparing Shenley Park with a similar park in China, this essay explores how an intercultural perspective deepens my understanding of both environments and the cultures they represent, as well as the assumptions I hold about them. Content: At first, the name Schenley Park gives a sense of refinement, perhaps a manicured space full of statues and formal gardens. The word “Schenley” conjures up images of an English park with cobblestone paths and elegant benches. My image of an English park also comes from the scene in the movie “Before Sunset” where Jesse and Céline take a walk along the Seine. I imagined a place where you can hear people casually discussing philosophy or poetry under the shade of ancient trees. In other words, despite the potential differences in style between the United States and the United Kingdom, Schenley Park was, in my first impression, a tranquil and beautiful place with an English park atmosphere.

Conflict: I have been to Schenley Park three times, and each time I went it gave me a different feeling. Reality was in stark contrast to my imagination. Instead of a well-designed formal garden, I saw a vast, open green space full of wild charm. And compared to a garden that is designed and maintained, Schenley Park is more like a vibrant animal. Because there has been no human maintenance of the man-made landscape for a long time. Many parts of the bridge have begun to rust, and the weeds along the road are growing luxuriantly.

Ascending movement: Compared to Chinese parks, Schenley Park has fewer artificial landscapes. I can't find the well-tended green spaces and paths, or the fitness equipment and small vendors in Chinese parks. From stone bridges to pagodas, the buildings not only shape the appearance of Chinese parks, but also create a self-consistent cultural atmosphere, allowing people to be in a designed natural beauty. However, the beauty of Schenley Park undoubtedly enhances the harmony and flow of nature, discarding too many artificial landscapes and returning the park to nature.

Climax: When comparing these two places, I realized that my initial assumptions about Schenley Park stemmed from my unfamiliarity with American parks, and also reflected how discarding the understanding of parks from movies and television outside of my cultural context affected my expectations of “parks.”

Resolution: I have always liked to be in direct contact with nature and get along with it. When I was in China, I often went hiking and mountain climbing. This may be the reason why I liked the lake area of Schenley Park the first time I visited. So when you come to a place with a different cultural background, it is necessary to try more and explore an unfamiliar area. In the short term, it may give me a direct cultural shock, but it also allows me to discover the delightful aspects of different cultures as I learn about them.

annotations.comment: 4 The outline looks nice! You may consider using your first language in the narrative to enrich its content. Overall, good job!
